title: Egg on Cotton
timeEstimate: 10–30 minutes (raw); heat-set egg climbs a tier
stainChemistry: Egg is mostly albumin protein (in the white) with a fat and pigment fraction in the yolk. Albumin is the textbook heat-coagulating protein — it cooks into an insoluble film on cotton the moment warm water touches raw egg, which is why eggs on shirts are fine until someone rinses with hot water. Yolk adds lecithin emulsifier and the yellow yellow-orange pigments. Cotton tolerates enzymes, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, and hot post-chemistry wash; the narrow window is that first flush.
whyThisWorks: Cold flush keeps the albumin soluble. Enzyme hydrolyzes the egg-white protein and frees whatever yolk fat the white was holding in the fiber. NSD emulsifies residual yolk oil. For any lingering yellow from the yolk pigment, 3% H₂O₂ oxidizes the yellow-orange pigments to colorless. Hot wash finishes. Reverse the order — hot water on raw egg — and the whole stain cooks into an insoluble film that chemistry can't reach.
defaultAssumption: Fresh raw egg on cotton is a high-confidence DIY. Heat-set egg (ironed, hot-washed, or cooked onto the fabric somehow — e.g., sitting on a warm car hood) climbs to pro territory: the albumin has lock together into a film.

spottingProtocol.technique: Lift yolk bulk, cold flush from back
spottingProtocol.equipment: Dull blade, cold water, white cotton terry towel
spottingProtocol.instruction: Scrape any solid yolk off with a dull blade. Cold flush from the BACK of the fabric. Cold only — warm water cooks the albumin instantly and locks the stain permanently.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Liquid Protein Spotter in warm water (30–38°C)
spottingProtocol.instruction: Apply Protein Spotter directly or dissolve enzyme pretreat in warm water. Enzyme window is warm, not hot. Dwell 15–20 min. Light brush after the first 5. protein-digesting enzyme hydrolyzes the albumin matrix.
Apply NSD, work with a soft brush. Emulsifies the yolk lipid residue. Rinse cool from the back.
For any residual yellow yolk pigment shadow: 3% H₂O₂ drop-by-drop. Oxidizes the yellow-orange pigments color. Dwell 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
Rinse until water runs clear. Launder hot per care label.

steps: 1. Scrape yolk bulk. Cold flush from the back.
2. Liquid Protein Spotter in warm water (30–38°C). Dwell 15–20 min.
3. NSD — emulsify yolk oil. Cool rinse.
4. 3% H₂O₂ on residual yellow pigment. 3–5 min.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cold flush; warm (30–38°C) for enzyme; cool through NSD + peroxide; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never warm-water-flush fresh egg — albumin cooks instantly
Enzyme window is warm, not hot — above 50°C the protein-digesting enzyme sets
One oxidizer at a time, NSD-flushed between

homeSolutions.agent: Cold Water, Not Warm — This One Is Absolute
homeSolutions.instruction: Of all the stains, egg on fabric has the narrowest window. Warm or hot water on raw egg cooks the white INSTANTLY into an insoluble film you can't remove at home. Cold water only. If you just dropped an egg on your shirt, get cold water on it in the next 60 seconds and the rest of this is easy.

homeSolutions.agent: Scrape Yolk, Flush From the Back
homeSolutions.instruction: Scrape any yolk off with a dull butter knife. Then hold the BACK of the stained area under cold running water. The egg pushes out the way it came in.

Use an enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout). Dissolve in WARM water per the label — hot kills the enzymes. Apply to the stain, let sit 15–20 minutes. The enzymes digest the egg-white protein.

homeSolutions.agent: Dawn + Soft Brush for Yolk Oil
homeSolutions.instruction: Work a drop of Dawn dish soap into the spot with a soft toothbrush. Dawn lifts the yolk's fat content. Rinse cool.

homeSolutions.agent: Hydrogen Peroxide 3% for Yellow Shadow (Whites Only)
homeSolutions.instruction: If a yellow shadow remains from the yolk: dab 3% drugstore hydrogen peroxide on the spot, let it bubble 3–5 min, blot. Skip on dark or dyed cotton.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia — toxic fume risk.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If you still see the stain, repeat the enzyme pass first.

If the egg has been through a hot wash or dryer before enzyme treatment, the albumin has cooked into the fiber and home chemistry plateaus. Take it to a dry cleaner. Tell them: "Egg on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / heat-set], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + Dawn + 3% hydrogen peroxide."

steps: 1. COLD water only while the stain is raw — hot water cooks egg white into an insoluble film.
2. Scrape yolk, cold flush from the BACK.
3. Enzyme pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, Zout) dissolved in WARM water. Dwell 15–20 min.
4. Dawn + soft toothbrush for yolk oil.
5. 3% hydrogen peroxide on yellow shadow (whites only).
6. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
products: Enzyme laundry pretreat (OxiClean Versatile, Biz, or Zout)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if egg has been through a hot wash or dryer before enzyme treatment — albumin is cooked in. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: COLD water only — warm cooks egg white permanently
Enzymes die above 50°C — warm water, not hot
Never mix peroxide with chlorine bleach or ammonia

neverDo: Never use warm water on raw egg — cooks the albumin instantly
Never skip the enzyme step on dried egg — water won't touch lock together protein
Never layer oxidizers — one at a time
Never dry with residual stain visible
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates enzymes, NSD, 3% H₂O₂, hot wash post-chemistry
Dyed cotton: skip or inside-seam-test peroxide

materialWarnings: COLD during spotting — warm cooks egg white permanently
Enzyme in warm water, not hot
Peroxide on whites; test inside seam on dark cotton

escalation.when: Egg heat-set by iron or hot dryer before enzyme treatment.
escalation.whatToTell: "Egg on cotton, age [fresh / overnight / heat-set], home-treated with enzyme pretreat + Dawn + 3% H₂O₂."

contextQuestions: Has the stain been rinsed with warm or hot water yet? (if yes, the chemistry is different — see heat-set path)
Raw egg or cooked (e.g., scrambled egg bit)?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Egg is the stain where ONE wrong move (warm water flush) turns an easy fix into a permanent problem. Temperature is the whole protocol.
